description:
You are an integration-developer who has just finished running a series of API tests.
        Three JSON log files were produced and are already present in

           /home/user/api_test/logs/

             ├── request_20240601.json
             ├── response_20240601.json
             └── error_20240601.json

        Your goal is to create a *single* compressed backup of these logs and to generate a
        tiny manifest that lists the filenames together with their exact byte-sizes.

        Required end-state of the system
        --------------------------------
        1. Directory:
              /home/user/api_test/backups
           must exist (create it if it does not).

        2. Compressed archive (GNU tar + gzip):
              /home/user/api_test/backups/logs_backup_20240601.tar.gz
           must exist and *must* contain the **three** files shown above
           under the internal directory path  logs/  (so, when you run
           “tar -tzf logs_backup_20240601.tar.gz” you see exactly:
                logs/request_20240601.json
                logs/response_20240601.json
                logs/error_20240601.json
           in that order).

        3. Manifest file:
              /home/user/api_test/backups/latest_backup_manifest.txt
           must exist, be world-readable, and have **exactly three lines** –
           one for each file inside the archive – using the following format
           (no leading/trailing spaces, no extra blank lines):

              <filename>|<size-in-bytes>

           where:
              • <filename> is the plain basename, e.g. request_20240601.json  
              • <size-in-bytes> is the decimal byte count you would obtain
                from “stat -c%s <file>” (or “wc -c”).

           Example of the expected layout (the numbers shown below are only
           illustrations – your values must match the real file sizes):

              request_20240601.json|18
              response_20240601.json|19
              error_20240601.json|14

        4. For easy human verification the agent should print the entire
           content of latest_backup_manifest.txt to STDOUT once the file
           has been created.

        Constraints
        -----------
        • Achieve the goal using a *simple* sequence of **three to four
          standard Linux commands** of your choice.
        • Do not hard-code the size values in the manifest; compute them
          from the actual files.
        • You do **not** need root privileges; everything happens under
          /home/user.
        • The archive must be compressed with gzip (i.e. “.tar.gz”), not
          plain tar.
        • The manifest must be placed **next to** the archive inside the
          backups directory, not inside the tarball.

        A correct solution will therefore leave the system with:
           - one directory (/home/user/api_test/backups)
           - one gzipped tar archive containing exactly the three log files
           - one manifest file whose three lines match the filename|size
             specification above.
        The automated checker will extract the tarball, verify its
        structure, and compare the manifest’s numeric byte counts to the
        real file sizes.

        Begin when ready. Remember to finish by echoing the manifest
        so it appears in the console output.
truth:
Pre-existing files *before* the task starts
        -------------------------------------------
        /home/user/api_test/logs/request_20240601.json
              content (18 bytes, **no trailing newline**):
                {"request":"ping"}
        /home/user/api_test/logs/response_20240601.json
              content (19 bytes, **no trailing newline**):
                {"response":"pong"}
        /home/user/api_test/logs/error_20240601.json
              content (14 bytes, **no trailing newline**):
                {"error":null}

        Items that must exist *after* successful completion
        ---------------------------------------------------
        1. Directory  /home/user/api_test/backups
           permissions: 755 (drwxr-xr-x)

        2. File       /home/user/api_test/backups/logs_backup_20240601.tar.gz
           • gzipped tar archive
           • internal paths (exact order):
                logs/request_20240601.json
                logs/response_20240601.json
                logs/error_20240601.json
           • Each member file’s contents must byte-for-byte match the
             originals listed above.

        3. File       /home/user/api_test/backups/latest_backup_manifest.txt
           permissions: 644 (-rw-r--r--)
           exact textual content (each line terminated by a single LF):
                request_20240601.json|18
                response_20240601.json|19
                error_20240601.json|14
           (no blank lines before/after).

        The automated grader will:
           • ensure the backups directory exists
           • gunzip & untar logs_backup_20240601.tar.gz
           • compare the extracted files’ SHA256 digests with the originals
           • compute the byte size of each extracted file
           • read latest_backup_manifest.txt
           • verify that the three lines appear **exactly** as shown above
             and that the numeric sizes correspond to the computed values.
